code_verification: receiver method exists on the receiver type
static methods are called statically and member methods are called on valid instances
assignment target is compatible with the method return type
argument count, argument type, and overload shape match the public API
enum owner and enum value both exist
property constants use the correct owner and value type
signal callback signatures match the public signal typedef
code blocks do not rely on inferred namespaces or overloads from naming alone
draft_requirements: Write enough practical detail for application developers to use the feature in a dali-ui app.
Ground natural-language behavior claims in the actual public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when the context pack is not enough.
Before writing examples, inspect the referenced public headers and relevant sample files when needed.
Prefer examples copied or adapted from existing samples when they match the feature; otherwise keep examples small and verify every API use.
After drafting, review the completed Markdown line by line against repository evidence and fix inaccurate explanations or code before returning.
repair_requirements: Repair only the problematic spans and the smallest surrounding context needed; do not rewrite the whole document.
For each listed issue, use the repository evidence and context pack to verify the corrected wording or code before returning.
If an issue reveals the same API misuse nearby, fix the nearby equivalent misuse too; do not make unrelated style rewrites.
After repair, re-check the edited lines against public headers, implementation files, samples, or UTCs when needed.

rules: Use dali-ui symbols as the app-facing API surface.
Use dali-core and dali-adaptor symbols only as setup/background unless the usage pattern explicitly marks them preferred.
Prefer View-based examples over raw Actor object-model examples.

usage_patterns.intent: Create and configure the primary object using public dali-ui APIs.
usage_patterns.usage_note: Prefer typed constructors and setters when available.

usage_patterns.intent: React to user or resource events through public signals.
usage_patterns.usage_note: Use signal connection APIs only with their owning trait or class.

rendered_prompt:
You are reviewing an existing DALi dali-ui application developer guide draft.
This is Phase 2.3.5: source-grounded prose review.

Primary goal:
- Improve natural-language accuracy and guide flow using repository evidence.
- Preserve the document's useful structure and examples whenever possible.
- Do not perform a full rewrite.
- Keep the revised user-facing guide in en only.
- Remove Korean, Japanese, Chinese, or other non-English prose from the revised guide.

Feature target:
- Feature: Interactive View (interactive-view)
- Category: views-components
- Guide profile: dali-ui-app
- Audience: application developers using dali-ui public APIs

Review rules:
- Review every natural-language paragraph line by line against public headers, implementation files, samples, and UTCs when needed.
- Accuracy is the first priority. Flow is the second priority.
- Edit the smallest unit needed: word, sentence, paragraph, or section-local addition/removal.
- If a word-level change is enough, change only the word.
- If a sentence-level change is enough, change only the sentence.
- If an entire paragraph is based on a wrong premise, revise, add, or remove that paragraph.
- Preserve code blocks when possible.
- If prose can be corrected to match an already accurate code block, prefer changing prose.
- If the code block itself makes the prose inaccurate or breaks the guide flow, rewrite only the smallest necessary part of that code block and verify it against public headers/source.
- Do not invent APIs.
- Do not add generic filler just to make the document longer.
- Do not change section structure unless prose_review.allow_section_restructure is true or the current prose is clearly misleading.
- Prefer localized edits when prose_review.localized_edits_only is true.
- Preserve code blocks especially when prose_review.preserve_code_blocks is true.
- Prefer Dali::Ui::View, typed setters, fluent chaining, and view-tree idioms for app-facing guidance.
- Treat dali-core and dali-adaptor APIs as reference/context unless the feature explicitly requires them.

Original Markdown draft:
---
title: Interactive View
sidebar_label: Interactive View
category: views-components
---

# Interactive View

`Dali::Ui::InteractiveView` is a `Dali::Ui::View` subclass for clickable, pressable UI elements in a dali-ui application.

## Table of Contents

- [Create an Interactive View](#create-an-interactive-view)
- [Handle Clicks](#handle-clicks)
- [Track Pressed State](#track-pressed-state)
- [Handle Long Presses](#handle-long-presses)
- [Control Clickability and Pseudo Disabled State](#control-clickability-and-pseudo-disabled-state)
- [Configure Key Activation](#configure-key-activation)
- [Use Interactive View Handles](#use-interactive-view-handles)
- [Fluent APIs for Derived Interactive Components](#fluent-apis-for-derived-interactive-components)

## Create an Interactive View

Use `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::New` when you need a plain interactive container. It returns an initialized `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView` handle. Because `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView` is a `Dali::Ui::View`, use it in your view tree like other dali-ui views.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

InteractiveView CreateInteractiveSurface()
{
  InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::New();

  view
    .SetClickable(true)
    .SetPseudoDisabled(false);

  return view;
}
```

`Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::SetClickable`, `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::SetPseudoDisabled`, and `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::SetKeyClickPolicy` return `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView&`, so they can be chained while configuring the object.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/key-click-policy.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

InteractiveView CreateKeyboardActivatedSurface()
{
  return InteractiveView::New()
    .SetClickable(true)
    .SetPseudoDisabled(false)
    .SetKeyClickPolicy(KeyClickPolicy::ON_RELEASE);
}
```

## Handle Clicks

Use `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::ClickedSignal` or the chaining helper `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::ConnectClickedSignal` to react when the view is clicked. The clicked callback receives the clicked `Dali::Ui::View` and the `Dali::Ui::InputEvent` that caused the click.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

class PanelController
{
public:
  InteractiveView CreatePanel()
  {
    InteractiveView panel = InteractiveView::New();

    panel.ConnectClickedSignal(this, &PanelController::OnPanelClicked);

    return panel;
  }

private:
  void OnPanelClicked(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    mLastClickedView = view;
  }

  View mLastClickedView;
};
```

`Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::ClickedSignal` returns a `Dali::Signal` reference with the callback shape `void(View, InputEvent)`.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

class ClickTracker
{
public:
  void Attach(InteractiveView view)
  {
    view.ClickedSignal().Connect(this, &ClickTracker::OnClicked);
  }

private:
  void OnClicked(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    mClicked = true;
    mClickedView = view;
  }

  bool mClicked{false};
  View mClickedView;
};
```

Multiple calls to `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::ConnectClickedSignal` add additional handlers.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

class ClickLogger
{
public:
  void Configure(InteractiveView view)
  {
    view
      .ConnectClickedSignal(this, &ClickLogger::RecordClick)
      .ConnectClickedSignal(this, &ClickLogger::UpdateSelection);
  }

private:
  void RecordClick(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    mClickCount += 1;
  }

  void UpdateSelection(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    mSelectedView = view;
  }

  int mClickCount{0};
  View mSelectedView;
};
```

## Track Pressed State

`Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::PressedChangedSignal` is emitted when the pressed state changes. Use it to update visual state or application state while a pointer, touch, key, or gesture interaction is active.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

class PressController
{
public:
  InteractiveView CreatePressableView()
  {
    return InteractiveView::New()
      .SetClickable(true)
      .ConnectPressedChangedSignal(this, &PressController::OnPressedChanged);
  }

private:
  void OnPressedChanged(View view, bool pressed, InputEvent event)
  {
    mPressedView = view;
    mPressed = pressed;
  }

  View mPressedView;
  bool mPressed{false};
};
```

You can query the current state with `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::IsPressed`.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

bool ShouldShowPressedFeedback(const InteractiveView& view)
{
  return view.IsClickable() && view.IsPressed();
}
```

## Handle Long Presses

Use `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::LongPressedSignal` or `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::ConnectLongPressedSignal` for long-press behavior. The callback shape is `bool(View, InputEvent)`. Return `true` to consume the long press and suppress the following click; return `false` to allow the click path to continue.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

class ContextMenuController
{
public:
  InteractiveView CreateMenuTarget()
  {
    return InteractiveView::New()
      .SetClickable(true)
      .ConnectLongPressedSignal(this, &ContextMenuController::OnLongPressed);
  }

private:
  bool OnLongPressed(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    mMenuTarget = view;
    return true;
  }

  View mMenuTarget;
};
```

Use `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::PressedChangedSignal` alongside `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::LongPressedSignal` when you need separate feedback for press-down, release, and long-press activation.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

class HoldController
{
public:
  InteractiveView CreateHoldTarget()
  {
    return InteractiveView::New()
      .ConnectPressedChangedSignal(this, &HoldController::OnPressedChanged)
      .ConnectLongPressedSignal(this, &HoldController::OnLongPressed);
  }

private:
  void OnPressedChanged(View view, bool pressed, InputEvent event)
  {
    mPressed = pressed;
  }

  bool OnLongPressed(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    mHeldView = view;
    return false;
  }

  bool mPressed{false};
  View mHeldView;
};
```

## Control Clickability and Pseudo Disabled State

Use `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::SetClickable` to allow or block click handling. Use `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::IsClickable` to inspect the current setting.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

void SetInteractionEnabled(InteractiveView view, bool enabled)
{
  view.SetClickable(enabled);
}

bool CanActivate(const InteractiveView& view)
{
  return view.IsClickable() && !view.IsPseudoDisabled();
}
```

Use `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::SetPseudoDisabled` for a disabled-like interaction state that can be observed with `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::IsPseudoDisabled`. `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::PseudoDisabledChangedSignal` is emitted when that state changes through `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::SetPseudoDisabled`.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

class AvailabilityController
{
public:
  InteractiveView CreateActionView()
  {
    InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::New();

    view.PseudoDisabledChangedSignal().Connect(
      this,
      &AvailabilityController::OnPseudoDisabledChanged);

    return view;
  }

  void SetAvailable(InteractiveView view, bool available)
  {
    view.SetPseudoDisabled(!available);
  }

private:
  void OnPseudoDisabledChanged(View view, bool pseudoDisabled)
  {
    mBlockedView = pseudoDisabled ? view : View();
  }

  View mBlockedView;
};
```

## Configure Key Activation

`Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::SetKeyClickPolicy` controls when key input produces a click for D-Pad, remote, or keyboard interaction. Use `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::GetKeyClickPolicy` to read the active policy.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/key-click-policy.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

InteractiveView CreateRemoteFriendlyView()
{
  return InteractiveView::New()
    .SetClickable(true)
    .SetKeyClickPolicy(KeyClickPolicy::ON_RELEASE);
}
```

Use `KeyClickPolicy::ON_PRESS` when activation should happen as soon as the key is pressed, `KeyClickPolicy::ON_RELEASE` when activation should happen on key release, and `KeyClickPolicy::DISABLED` when key input should not emit clicks.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/key-click-policy.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

void ConfigureForInputMode(InteractiveView view, bool activateOnPress, bool acceptKeyClicks)
{
  if(!acceptKeyClicks)
  {
    view.SetKeyClickPolicy(KeyClickPolicy::DISABLED);
  }
  else if(activateOnPress)
  {
    view.SetKeyClickPolicy(KeyClickPolicy::ON_PRESS);
  }
  else
  {
    view.SetKeyClickPolicy(KeyClickPolicy::ON_RELEASE);
  }
}

bool ActivatesOnPress(const InteractiveView& view)
{
  return view.GetKeyClickPolicy() == KeyClickPolicy::ON_PRESS;
}
```

## Use Interactive View Handles

Use `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView::DownCast` when you receive a generic handle and need to work with interactive-view-specific APIs.

```cpp
#include <dali/public-api/object/base-handle.h>
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali;
using namespace Dali::Ui;

void ConfigureIfInteractive(BaseHandle handle)
{
  InteractiveView view = InteractiveView::DownCast(handle);

  if(view)
  {
    view
      .SetClickable(true)
      .SetPseudoDisabled(false);
  }
}
```

`Dali::Ui::InteractiveView` handles can be copied, assigned, and moved like other dali-ui handles. Copying the handle does not create a new view object; it gives another handle to the same object.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

class ViewStore
{
public:
  void Store(InteractiveView view)
  {
    mStoredView = view;
  }

  bool StoredViewIsClickable() const
  {
    return mStoredView && mStoredView.IsClickable();
  }

private:
  InteractiveView mStoredView;
};
```

## Fluent APIs for Derived Interactive Components

`interactive-view.autogen.h` provides `DALI_UI_CHAIN_INTERACTIVEVIEW_METHODS`, the generated macro used by dali-ui interactive component classes to expose the same fluent methods as `Dali::Ui::InteractiveView`.

The generated chaining surface includes `SetPseudoDisabled`, `SetClickable`, `SetKeyClickPolicy`, `ConnectClickedSignal`, `ConnectPressedChangedSignal`, and `ConnectLongPressedSignal` for a derived child class. Application code normally consumes those methods from the public component type rather than including `interactive-view.autogen.h` directly.

```cpp
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/interactive-view.h>
#include <dali-ui-foundation/public-api/key-click-policy.h>

using namespace Dali::Ui;

class InteractionFactory
{
public:
  InteractiveView CreateConfiguredView()
  {
    return InteractiveView::New()
      .SetClickable(true)
      .SetPseudoDisabled(false)
      .SetKeyClickPolicy(KeyClickPolicy::ON_RELEASE)
      .ConnectClickedSignal(this, &InteractionFactory::OnClicked)
      .ConnectPressedChangedSignal(this, &InteractionFactory::OnPressedChanged)
      .ConnectLongPressedSignal(this, &InteractionFactory::OnLongPressed);
  }

private:
  void OnClicked(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    mClickedView = view;
  }

  void OnPressedChanged(View view, bool pressed, InputEvent event)
  {
    mPressed = pressed;
  }

  bool OnLongPressed(View view, InputEvent event)
  {
    mLongPressedView = view;
    return true;
  }

  View mClickedView;
  View mLongPressedView;
  bool mPressed{false};
};
```
